sections.title: Chairman's Chat
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

My flying thus far this year has been something of a learning experience, both re-gaining currency after the winter lay-off, and adjusting to new kit.  After a local session of ground handling and leading a tour of the sites around Hawes, my first proper flight took place at Semer (N) at the end of February.  I include reports on all my Dales flights in the flight log on forum.

Next stop Morocco – a social 10-day trip with old friends and, with plenty of coastal soaring, a chance to regain currency in a fairly benign environment.  I’ve still not made my mind up about Morocco and, just like last year, I haven’t decided whether I’ll go back.  I wrote about last year’s trip in the April ’24 edition of Skywords.

Being a tight arse (and proud of it!) I don’t generally buy new gear.  In a period of just over 12 months I have upgraded almost my entire kit; a 10 year old wing, a second hand harness that I have been using for 6 years (thanks Simon), and a reserve that is identical to the old one, but 10 years younger.  I’m currently flying Ed’s old Flow Fusion, and paid Kev’s family (via Ed) for his old harness, helmet and radio.

Despite flying the Fusion (EN C, 2.5 liner) for most of last season, it’s the first time that I’d flown it in Morocco.  I’m not quite sure if it was the thermic conditions or the increased performance, but I found top landing this year more challenging than on my old Rush 4 (EN B) last year.  Lots of safe overshoots (buildings and vehicles on the undershoot) and one unintentional full stall about 4 feet off the ground, which I got away with.  Mercifully we didn’t get to play in the dunes so the Porcher Skytex wasn’t exposed to too much of the dreaded abrasive salty sand.

Kev’s open face helmet was great, but I found that I couldn’t hear the radio through the headset.  A close inspection under the lining revealed that the speaker was positioned near the top of the helmet.  Since my bald patch isn’t very good at listening, I moved the speaker next to my ear, which has marginally better performance.  Next problem was that it was seriously deafening (perhaps why Kev moved it).  To my horror, having studied the manual and searched the forums, I discovered that you can’t set and lock the volume, which is very sensitive and easily knocked.  Seems strange for such a sophisticated, if old, Alinco radio.  I’m now experimenting with positioning it on my flight deck for easy access.  

The last 4 days in Morocco were subject to strong northerlies.  We had the option to drive (5 hours) but decided to do the sort of thing that most ‘normal’ people do on holiday instead.  Good job I’d milked the first 5 days for all they were worth: 12 hours in total.

Back to the UK with nice soft grass rather than sand and rocks, it was time to try Kev’s old harness (Advance Lightness 3).  I’d forgotten that it comes with its own reserve inner bag, and I didn’t find closing the outer container particularly intuitive, so installing the reserve took a bit longer than I anticipated.  It’s also the first time that I’ve tried a soft link connector – I like it!  Getting the wing and harness into the rucksack was also a new experience: I’m not sure which was more stressed the first time – me or the rucksack zips!

I fell in love with the harness before I’d even flown it.  The carry up Mallerstang, with my new walking poles, was a joy that I haven’t experienced for some time.  Strapping into the harness comes with a couple of extra stages and requires a bit more thought and time, but I quickly fell into the routine.  In the air I decided that I prefer a hammock to a seat board, even managing to ‘left hand thermal’ up to cloudbase!  I started off using the harness settings as Kev had left them, and despite sitting in a makeshift harness hanger beforehand, I found that, after an hour in the air, the heel of the pod was too short.

Having adjusted the bottom lines to their maximum length, my next proper flight was at Wether Fell.  The heel of the pod was still too short and I worried that I’d bought the wrong size.  I decided to ignore the manual, which suggests adjusting the diagonal lines first (to get the pod level).  I’ve slackened those off and it now seems much better, without lowering the angle of the pod too much.  I’m looking forward to my next air test.

As many people have said before, changing gear isn’t trivial, and you need to allow yourself time to adjust.

sections.title: Coaching
sections.content:
Pete Logan, Chief Coach reports

I had a couple of reports of air sickness from the Easter weekend flying at Whenside. I put this down to spring thermals mixing with a fairly sharp inversion that was showing for the 20th April - red line ~3000ft or 900hPa level….

A rising packet of air does not “know” it’s going to encounter an inversion on the way up so generally hits it at full speed and then chaotic fluid dynamics governs what happens next. As you can imagine, this leads to a fair amount of air swirling around, making us uncomfortable. You know if you’re in it. Generally you feel like there’s lots of blips and bubbles but it’s very hard to establish a climb further. If your climb has a lot of inertia, maybe it punches a way through but it certainly slows down.

Those with a keen eye for a skew T will also notice the wind direction changes from easterly low down to southerly by 8000ft. That height was probably above the boundary layer for the day so I can’t be sure that it would have had an effect but certainly it won’t have been helping. There is a marked 45 degree veer in the wind direction at the inversion height and that will have been mixing things up quite a bit as well. We know it was there because looking at Rahul’s track it curves around from S to N as he gets higher and it’s pretty SE to NW anyway….

So, spring thermals being forced to change direction at the same time as they’re hitting a barrier. Small wonder people were wanting to blow chunks.

I’ll leave the medical side of dealing with it to those who know but I have experienced air sickness once before and I put that down to lack of sleep and job worries at the time. I put into practise some of the recommendations below and managed to salvage a lovely day’s flying out of it.

There'll be a lot of unnecessary movement that can be tuned out by:

1, Seatboarded harness. Less hip rhumba is needed to damp out the brief roll movements as you move through turbulent air. Obviously not something you can do immediately, but if you’re prone to air sickness and have a hammock harness then it should inform your next purchase.

2, Tighten the chest strap narrower than the recommended max. This will damp out roll and yaw. If you need to tighten in flight, wait for some steady air, sit forward, hands off the brakes, wrap your right arm around the outside of the right riser and grab the left riser with your right hand to pull it in. Left hand can then more easily tighten the retainer on the chest strap.

3, When thermalling, your outside brake should be used to control the outside tip pitch to minimise the little dives. I’m still working on that one myself but it is one of the ways that you can carve the turns in a

4, Keep thermal turns circular. It may not make you the most popular flying with others but circles are more predictable compared with extending into lift and then throwing a tight turn back in. You'll lose in climb efficiency but you'll feel like you want to be in the air for longer.

5, I don’t know that this works but it feels like it should, so… switch to a pod harness. A sit harness will keep your head higher above the axis of rotation for roll and pitch so there will be more movement and acceleration to cope with. Getting your eyes and ears as closer to the carabiners will probably help to reduce the sensation. From this perspective the horizon won’t be moving as much which will help keep your eyes on it. Maybe just believe this last point and the placebo effect will help out anyway.

Note that tips 1 & 2 are going to reduce your ability to hunt climbs and feel the air but you'll tune yourself to be more sensitive to lift signals. I hope this helps is you find yourself suffering.

sections.title: Sites and News
sections.content:
Tom Shuttleworth, Sites Officer (North)

The last photo Billy ever took after he bombed out on the moor.   [Actually, AI image generation is getting good!]

We enjoy lots of support, and have all experienced the comments and questions of those who find joy (and bewilderment) in seeing our wings in the air.  But I’m aware of a couple of recent concerns raised by farmers (Whernside) and gamekeepers (Nappa).  This is not unusual - particularly at this time of year (lambing and the start of ground-nesting season), and it’s the job of your Sites Officers to find out what’s happened, to communicate with relevant parties, and to see if there are things we could be doing differently or better. Ultimately, we want to keep the sites open so that people have as many options as possible to fly.  

It’s encouraging that in both instances I was made aware almost immediately, and that good relationships enabled swift contact with the right people to give assurances.  So this is simply one of the regular reminders:  

1) Read the site guides before you fly.  They are updated regularly, so even if you have flown the site a thousand times, it’s important to check if anything has changed.

2) Be sensitive to those who own, live and work on the land where we fly.  They are often under pressures we don’t always understand very well.  

3) Please let me or Stephen know if there are any issues as soon as you can (check the relevant site guide if you are not sure who to contact).  

I’m really grateful for the support of those living close to sites, or people who know the landowners, farmers and keepers better than I do - they are often the first to know of potential problems, and it’s highly valued.  If you could also help in this way for any of our sites, please let us know.

sections.title: BP Cup Dales Round 2025
sections.content:
Rob Urselmann reports

The BPCup is a “UK based paragliding competition for pilots in the non-skygod category. Our aims are – flying, fun and learning – in that order!. It is a friendly four-day competition which gives you an opportunity to hone the skills needed to fly XC successfully”.  You must be Pilot rated and a BHPA member, and fly an EN-C or below wing.

This was the second year I participated in the Dales Round, and I quite enjoyed catching up with people I had only ever met on last year’s Dales Round as well as a handful of our local DHPC pilots.  It’s a bit of a family get-together feeling.  Last year my personal aim was just to learn how this competition thing worked, this year my aim was to improve my decision making and get to Goal without pressuring myself on speed.

Day 1, Thursday 24 April, gave us an excellent ESE-SE forecast, so 48 pilots, one race director and 2 dogs trotted up Whernside. Like last year during the Whernside task then, several ‘southerners’ moaned again about the length of the walk-in and the steepness of the hill.  And we were only on the low end of Whernside!  Initially the wind was still quite high, so we settled down on the ledge below the ridge for a spot of para-waiting.

As the wind started to improve, a 33.5km elapsed time Task was set to a Goal centred on Sour Howes (a hill East of Troutbeck), with a 5km radius and end of speed section (ESS) 1km further out. Being elapsed time, the time counted was from (last time) leaving the Start Cylinder to reaching ESS instead of first to arrive, and the size of cylinder allowed (in theory) for a civilised landing near the A591 and Windermere.   By briefing time the wind had settled right down and, having already checked it out, several of us scooted up the last bit of the hill to set up right at the top.

As I took off into the 1km radius Start cylinder, I just glanced that it was 12:44 when XCTrack automatically switched to the Thermal screen, and for some reason left me in a bit of a panic that I might depart the cylinder before Race. Once safe soaring and in calmer air I swiped back to Task screen and saw to my relief that now 12:46 and I was still well inside the cylinder. The fact I could at any time before 15:00 simply loop back into the Start cylinder and reset the clock had evaded me for a minute. Call it race nerves. Anyhow, the wind still being a bit east, I followed the earlier starters toward the top bowl where pilots were already climbing high.

Task1 Whernside (photo Ed Cleasby)

Then, before I even got to the bowl, I found a nice thermal, climbed to over 1000m (3300’) to base and was looking along a nice line of dark cloud straight down the race line. I remembered the 13:00 rasp forecast was for a nice convergence line which would later move on and be replaced by a big blue (sink?) hole over that area so, although there was nobody else with me and nobody had left yet, I turned and went downwind. I cruised over Gt Coum and on to Barkin where I caught a thermal in the middle of the valley and 13:16 I arrived over Barkin ridge at 840m, well above ridge height. However, not comfortable with that to continue I soared above the ridge line waiting for another good thermal and then other pilots started to appear and passed by full speed well overhead, seemingly effortlessly staying high. I flew to the Calf Top hoping for more thermic action there being both the high point on Barkin and more under the cloud line, and sure enough I had a nice climb and at 13:29 was back at 1050m and onward I went. Now I had pilots in front of me to aim for as they picked up thermals: the bottom of the lune valley, dark fields, Killington Reservoir, a quarry. However, with the geo wind dropped down, thermals being only 0.5-1.5m/s and base gradually dropping, overall progress seemed good to me in XC but, further to our left and seemingly out of reach, pilots kept flying by in what seemed a straight line high under the darkest bit of the clouds.

Then a spot of entertainment was provided by a quad of F15’s first flying SW well below us, to return a while later after their tour-du-Lakes and starting to sound rather loud. A few seconds later, as I felt the jet noise rumbling in my seat, I spotted one single F15 flying away from above the cloud I was under. I heard later the first three had stayed low but one had high-tailed it right over top of us.  These are the moments I convince myself that those high-tech jets secretly really do notice and triangulate our electronic conspicuity Flarm signals and the pilots know exactly where we are….

 

BOLO: Be On Look Out (photo Joshua Coombs)

Back to the Task at hand and now a bit NE of Kendal, the wind shifted more S’ly and larger patches of blue skies appeared. With an increasingly appalling sink rate, I aimed for the high ground and reached the shallow slopes at soaring height, barely 3km from Goal. I sniffed around a few weak lifts but was already in landing mode when in a last ditch attempt I reached Gurnal Dubs reservoir. I found just enough climb to get to me to the west slope and in my mind able to land a bit further down when I hit a whopper +4m/s bang on. Very quickly changing gears, I cranked up, hung on and 2.5 minutes later I was back at 900m and 500+m AGL and easily in reach of ESS and Goal. Fuelled by optimism again, I figured to tag ESS & Goal and then continue on toward Grasmere. Why not?!   However, as I lost 100m on glide the ground below me climbed up and as I entered the Kentmere valley the air turned outright turbulent and rather nasty for my liking. With both my own and very recently another pilot’s accident fresh in mind, I fairly quickly called quits, slid out sideways to the open ground west of Kentmere and at 14:55 I safely touched down next to a track … in the middle of nowhere. Nonetheless: GOAL!  Buoyed by having reached Goal and the ground sloping gently downhill on a good track turning road, the 2.5mile hike out to Ings was rather pleasant. The day was complete when 0.5m short of Ings a kind lady stopped and gave me a lift to Kendal, where I got picked up by a Cup retrieve car.

Being away from the hill early I picked up 113 lead points, but with the winner doing the task in 1:06hr, taking 1:51hr elapsed time I had definitely taken the ‘scenic’ route on race day and came 20th out of 21 pilots reaching Goal. Still for me it was a Grand Day out.  [tracklog: https://xcleague.com/xc/flights/20251088.html?vx=10]

View to Task 1 Goal, with my landing spot arrowed (photo Ed Cleasby)

Day 2, Friday 25 April, gave a high-end SE-SSE, so the call was made for Barkin South with a late start. Those poor Southerners who thought Whernside S was steep now had cause for a real good huff&puff as a long line of big bags trotted up the long steep slope. At the top we found Ed Cleasby, who (as so often for the BPCup) had already been out scouting since the early hours to aid with site choice. Still being windy (and much chillier than the day before), the group hunkered down waiting for the wind to settle. After a while a single wing came over from Whernside way so we called on radio and, sure enough, Chris Kay (who else) responded with an in-air update: flyable but ‘odd’ air. So we waited a bit longer, until eventually Ed got seriously itchy feet and, sacrificing his race, took off as wind dummy.  Whilst maybe still a bit gusty on take-off point it was ok out front, so the Task was called: a 31.9km Race to Goal to Shap.

Having adjusted my XCTtrack Thermal screen by splitting it in half and adding the Task map, this time no issue exiting the start cylinder close on time although I had launched too late to also have climbed out and be high at start time.  Then I made a very poor choice, and instead of flying out the middle of the valley I took a climb at Calf Top in front of the ridge. Only to fall out of it at 850m and end up in bad sink behind the ridge line. Seeing I might not be able to pull forward again, I decided to be safe, quickly turned tail down and slid down over the back ridge toward the A683 road. “Great, that’s me being first on the deck!”. However, as I slid over the bottom gulley just behind the field I’d picked for landing, I hit a rocky but steady thermal turning into a solid one right back to base. “Yes! Back in the game!”.   Now, whilst my thermal drift had been smack on the rum line to Goal, I was now over the A683 west of Holme Knott and my original plan to try a route over the Howgills now seemed well off track, and the dark line of clouds to my left also well away. Instead I was smack on course for Greyrigg so I put my foot down and went for it. As I approached Greyrigg, two pilots had already landed there (Ed Cleasby and free-flying John H) but I managed to get to the little bowl by the skin of my teeth. As I soared it hoping for a decent thermal to give me enough height to safely go over the sheer cliff back, about half a dozen other pilots joined from various directions. Some managed to find a “good enough” thermal and went over the back with minimal height (only to end up stuck on the next ridge), but three of us never got away. After an hour soaring and a sky that was only further shutting down, a bit after 4pm I called it quits and went to land near the road. As I did so the rest promptly followed, and one or two other pilots even appeared from somewhere else and landed short of the hill to also join us. My good mate Alan Oliver, having had a less happy day before, had managed to take the Howgills route and via that way had made it to 21km ending up with several of the top pilots, so he was a very happy bunny indeed. Well Done!!

By the time I was packed up Viv had already arranged transport and within minutes a retrieve van pulled up which we all piled into. Service!  I had left my car at Kirkby Lonsdale’s Devil’s Bridge, so from there turned tail and went back to Sedbergh to retrieve a few more pilots, dropping one of at Barkin to drive back another pilots car and picking up more in the pub in Kirkby and we all made it to Camp in time for the BBQ.   I didn’t make goal (only 5 did) and was a bit annoyed with myself for my early mistake, but I had again had fun, learned lots and all in it was another Grand Day out. [tracklog: https://xcleague.com/xc/flights/20251143.html?vx=10]

Task 2 (photo Martin Bastow)

Task 1 multi-track (compiled by David McLeod)

        SportsTrackLive: https://www.sportstracklive.com/en/scene/1bdae8c2-c6ec-4fec-8cee-724951e5ae90/ 

Ogoy:  https://ogoy.app/player?scene=qlirtlbag1hxfcc5xjnd 

Task 2 Ogoy multi-track (compiled by Robin Wallace) https://ogoy.app/player?scene=s9666lxdgybipecky423 

Well deserved BBQ, author stuffing his face (photo Ehsan Baninajar)

Team photo
        

Day 3, Saturday 26th, with a high-end, claggy and full overcast day looked “flyable but not really taskable” so the day got canned from a Task perspective. Many went to Stags, a few of us to Cotterside. Being kinda still in competition mode, I decided to push myself and despite it being a very grey day I set a rather optimistic 30km Goal at Leyburn, a route I’ve always fancied but never done. To my amazement, I made it 21.8km to the point at Woodhall where lack of any thermals stopped progress. It was only as I write this that I realise I could have turned around and see how far I could fly back, silly me. Still, another and unexpected Grand Day. [tracklog: https://xcleague.com/xc/flights/20251159.html?vx=10]

That evening the BPCup had evening lectures on XC and competition mindset, where I picked up several good snippets and including Robin Wallace’s “46 tips for Paraglide Racing for Beginners” [https://youtu.be/oFPyC3pBEAc] and the notion of “haze caps” being (sometimes) visible as bubbles at an otherwise blued out inversion layer to indicate location of thermals.

If you are keen to improve your XC flying skills in a fun and supportive environment, I can thoroughly recommend doing the BPCup. There are still spaces on this years Greece Round 14-19 September https://airtribune.com/bpcup-2025-sopot-round and a few spots in the preceding Guided week (only for pilots also in the comp)

sections.title: Social News
sections.content:
Stef Sykes reports

As we head into the flying season, we abate our monthly meet-ups until the autumn as we’ll all see each other ‘on the hill’ and share banter and lessons there.

To note two things then, from me….

The first is that our next formal social is the Dales Fly In 2025 - the fifth such annual event, and, as ever, shall feature the ever marvellous X Dales Hike and Fly. This takes place over the first weekend of September, being the 5th to the 7th. Up until the end of this month, there are discounted tickets at £25 especially for DHPC members. Tickets are available from here https://www.eventbrite.co.uk/e/dales-fly-in-2025-tickets-1316023946049

The second is that your club is looking for a new Social Secretary from December this year. I will be standing down at the next AGM. If you’re interested in the most fun role on the committee, do get in touch with me at social@dhpc.org.uk

Happy Flying

sections.title: From the Archive - ‘Nightmare in Owens’
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

This one from 1993 is dedicated to Kev Gay, long time hang glider pilot, who never misses the opportunity to tell us how dangerous paragliding must be.  It’s all relative I suppose.

sections.title: Library
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

The contents of the club library are published on the website here.  In the absence of club nights over the summer period there isn’t much opportunity to exchange books or DVDs.  Once Mike has a roof over his head and has re-assumed his post as librarian, he’s promised to look into the prospect of offering a postal service.  In the meantime if you want to reserve anything for the September club night then drop me a line: chairman@dhpc.org.uk

sections.title: Safety
sections.content:
Clive Sury reports

Springtime Soaring: Navigating the Dynamic Skies of the UK

I’ve heard a few reports of people experiencing collapses and cravats so far this year in the spring thermals.  Indeed, from last week an incident report has been raised from flying at Windbank and also, one recently from Wether Fell.  Plus, on my last flights at Whernside and Windbank in the last week I’ve found the wind to be highly variable.  Therefore, I’ve sourced this article below to help people consider the implications of spring thermals and how to navigate them.

This article has been sourced from adventuro - The Home of Adventure Sports who are a website that links to a plethora of activity sport trips.

The Meteorological Mood of Spring

Spring in the UK is a season of transition and contrast. The increasing strength of the sun warms the Earth unevenly, leading to some unique atmospheric conditions that can dramatically affect paragliding:

  • Strong Solar Heating: As the sun climbs higher, its rays become more effective at heating the ground. This creates powerful thermals that can lift a paraglider swiftly and significantly.
  • Cool Air: Despite the warmer sun, the air can still be quite cool, especially at altitude. This temperature difference between the ground and the air can intensify thermal activity, making the thermals particularly strong and sharp-edged.
  • Drying Terrain: Winter’s moisture slowly dissipates or is absorbed, reducing surface drag and allowing the sun’s heat to more directly affect the ground, thus enhancing thermal generation.

These conditions result in punchy thermals surrounded by areas of rapidly sinking air. This creates a challenging environment for all pilots, particularly those who are shaking off the winter rust.

subsections.title: Spring Flying Safety Tips
subsections.content:
Assess Your Readiness

Before you take to the skies, honestly evaluate your experience and how current your skills are. Spring’s robust conditions are not ideal for a casual re-entry after a winter hiatus. If it’s been a while since you last flew, consider some refresher flights in milder conditions to rebuild your muscle memory.

Observe and Learn

Watch how other pilots are handling the conditions. Notice any pitching, rolling, or yawing of their wings. This can give you a real-time sense of the air’s behaviour. If it looks challenging from the ground, it will certainly be more so in the air.

Tactical Turning

Be wary of performing 360 turns close to the hill. The narrow, vigorous spring thermals can abruptly eject you into sinking air. Maintain a substantial safety margin before committing to any maneuvers that may put you at risk of rapid altitude loss.

Active Flying Is Crucial

Once airborne, focus intently on flying your wing. Spring thermals require active piloting, especially near the ground where turbulence is common. Keep your hands on the brakes and avoid distractions such as adjusting instruments or cameras. Pilots using pod harnesses should be particularly cautious, keeping their legs prepared for potential turbulence-induced collapses.

Prepared for Landing

Landing in spring can be tricky due to the unpredictable air. Get ready well in advance and be prepared for a Parachute Landing Fall (PLF) if you encounter a sudden drop or a collapse as you approach the ground. This technique can help minimise injury during a hard landing.

Maintain Distance

Keep a safe distance from the ridge while soaring. This extra space is crucial for reacting to sudden sink or for executing a necessary turn away from terrain, ensuring you have adequate clearance from the hill.

Identifying Turbulent Conditions in Spring

  1. Rapid Thermal Development:
  • In spring, the increasing strength of the sun heats the Earth unevenly, leading to powerful thermal activity. Look for signs of rapid thermal development, such as sudden cloud formation, particularly cumulus clouds, which can grow quickly and indicate strong updrafts that may lead to turbulence.
  1. Wind Shear and Gusts:
  • Spring winds can be gusty and changeable, with significant wind shear (a sudden change in wind speed and/or direction over a short distance vertically or horizontally). This can create choppy, turbulent air, particularly noticeable during transitions from calm to gusty conditions.
  1. Surface Indicators:
  • Pay attention to the movement of trees, grass, and surface water. These can provide real-time indicators of the wind conditions at lower levels. Sudden changes in the intensity of movement can signal incoming gusts or shifts in wind direction that could lead to turbulence.
  1. Obstacle-Induced Turbulence:
  • As winds increase in spring, they interact more aggressively with natural and man-made obstacles like hills, cliffs, and buildings. These interactions can produce turbulent rotor zones on the leeward sides of these obstacles. Be especially cautious in these areas.

What to Look for in Wind Forecasts

  1. Wind Speed and Gust Factor:
  • Examine both the average wind speeds and the gust factors. In spring, gusts can be significantly higher than the average wind speed, creating conditions ripe for turbulence. A high gust factor indicates unstable air and potentially challenging flying conditions.
  1. Directional Stability:
  • Check the forecast for changes in wind direction. Frequent shifts can indicate unsettled weather and increased risk of turbulence. Steady wind directions tend to be associated with more stable conditions.
  1. Thermal Forecasts:
  • Some advanced weather services provide thermal forecasts that predict the strength and distribution of thermals. Strong, narrow thermals can be particularly turbulent, especially if they are spaced closely together, leading to complex aerial environments.
  1. Local Weather Phenomena:
  • Be aware of local weather phenomena that might influence turbulence. For instance, valley breezes, sea breezes, or specific frontal activities can all impact the conditions differently, depending on your geographic area.

Additional Tips

  • Use Technology: Leverage apps and tools that provide real-time wind and weather data specifically tailored for paragliders. These can offer updates that might not be available in general weather forecasts.
  • Consult Local Coaches: If you are feeling a little rusty, then do not take-off first. Watch someone more experienced to take the lead and watch their experience before deciding whether it is for you.
  • Continuously Monitor: Conditions can change quickly in spring. Continuous monitoring of the sky and weather updates during your flying day is essential to stay ahead of any adverse changes.

Safe flying,

Clive.

sections.title: Airspace
sections.content:
Martin Baxter reports

Once every 6 months I attend a meeting at RAF Leeming.  Participants include aviators from every discipline that you can think of plus Air Traffic Controllers.  It goes by the catchy title of Regional Airspace User Working Group (RAUWG).  You can view my notes from previous meetings in the safety section of the club forum.

30 Apr 25

Introduction

The aim of each RAUWG is to reduce the risk of a mid-air-collision (MAC) between military and civilian airspace users within its region.

We live in uncertain times highlighting the important role that defence has to play. RAF Leeming is a NATO priority 1 airbase: in addition to routine training and exercises, it might also be used as a base for operations.

Air Safety Team

On 17 Oct 24 there was an AIRPROX between an R44 helicopter and 2 x Typhoons over East Witton, near Middleham, at approx. 1,600’ AMSL. The R44 was in contact with Leeming ATC but they failed to inform the Typhoons who were descending to low level. The helicopter pilot commented that in addition to the potential MAC he was concerned about the effects of downwash.

All pilots were asked to establish radio communications when operating in the vicinity of the airfield, and especially before entering the MATZ. They have been made aware that we don’t carry radios or transponders. I informed them that we are all being encouraged to use PureTrack but as with OGN, Flarm, etc, the sources are not assured and regulation prevents them using this information for deconfliction purposes. The biggest limitation is that people forget to turn their devices off when not flying!

CAA (DAATM)

You may recall mention of the ‘Atypical Air Environment’: the CAA proposal to limit commercial drones to follow routes along linear features such as pylon lines (the rationale being that nobody else flies there). There has been some kickback from commerce seeking to increase lateral and vertical limits.

DRAKEN

Draken is a civilian company that simulates enemy aircraft to facilitate RAF/RN training, with a particular emphasis on jamming. They operate jets (Falcon & L159s) out of Teesside which mostly operate above FL150. Of note they use a DA42 (twin propellor aircraft – see photo) to simulate ground attack down to 500’ AGL. If you see a NOTAM with wording along the lines of ‘pilots will be conducting high energy manoeuvres and may not be able to comply with the RAC (Rules of the Air)’ it’s likely to be them. There is no expectation that you will avoid the area.

                                                          

ATC

RAF Leeming will be moving from QFE to QNH meaning that circuit heights will be slightly higher: Visual circuit 1200’ QNH (within ATZ); Radar Training Circuit 2,700’ QNH (within MATZ).

There is an ACP in place to remove the MATZ at Topliffe. At present radio equipped aircraft are asked to request crossing. From our perspective it is likely to be a lot safer to transit the Topcliffe MATZ than the Leeming MATZ.

LBA ATC commented that the decision about DSA airspace was entirely political, and has already been made. Expect an announcement after the local elections.

 

11 Sqn (Hawk)

We received a similar briefing as previously. I noted their comment that if they saw paragliding NOTAMs in the Lake District then they would avoid the area.

Yorkshire Air Ambulance

Three aircraft to guarantee 2 serviceable, based at Topcliffe and Nostel Priory (Leeds). Tasked by Yorkshire Ambulance Service. When directed to a casualty they can cope with most types of coordinates and are familiar with What3Words. They asked about weather reports and I directed them to our remote weather station: Holfuy.

Gliding

Our area is particularly busy with sailplanes due to the unrestricted airspace, with airfields at Sutton Bank, Pocklington, Rufforth and Burn. Sutton Bank & Pocklington are sites of national importance and Rufforth is a site of regional importance. The focus at Sutton Bank is on towing whilst that at Rufforth is on aerotow and motor gliders, with the aim of connecting with wave where conditions allow. Sutton Bank is open 364 days with up to 20K movements per year. The height record is 33,000’ and distance record is 760km. Top sailplanes have a glide ratio of 50:1.

I was particularly struck by a comment from Keith Batty from Rufforth. ‘Defence will always trump free flight; Safety will always trump free flight; and Efficiency (Airspace and constant descent profiles) will often trump free flight: BUT we still need to protect the freedom to enjoy our sport’.

RAF Safety Centre

The RAF safety magazine, AIRCLUES, is well worth a scan, as is the CAA’s Skyway Code if you want easy access to check an aspect of flying regulations.

sections.title: Are You a Risk-Seeker? Thoughts on Flying, Fun, and Staying in One Piece
sections.content:
Joseph Edmonds postulates, with a bit of help from Google Gemini

Let's be honest, many of us are drawn to paragliding precisely because it involves an element of risk. I count myself among those who enjoy that thrill, that slight edge. But there's a world of difference between calculated adventure and reckless abandon. This article is a bit of a "brain dump" – some thoughts I’ve been mulling over about the kinds of risks we take, how they make us feel, and crucially, how we can manage them to keep flying, having fun, and avoiding the worst outcomes (death and injury being top of that list, with inconvenience a distant third!).

subsections.title: The Risks We Take in Paragliding
subsections.content:
When we take to the skies, we're constantly taking risks. Some are undeniably serious:

  • The risk of a significant fall.
  • The risk of mid-air collisions with other pilots or aircraft.
  • The risk of flying into obstacles.
  • The risk of breaking rules – entering controlled airspace or landing in prohibited areas.
  • The risk of a bad landing out, perhaps in a remote spot, leading to a long, arduous walk with heavy gear, or even the more serious risk of not being able to get back to civilisation easily.

These are all part of the paragliding landscape, but how we approach them defines our experience and our longevity in the sport.

Type 1 vs. Type 2 Risks: Knowing the Difference (and the Laziness Factor)

I’ve started thinking about risks in two broad categories, which helps me decide what I'm willing to accept:

Type 1 Risks: The Game Changers (and Enders)


These are the risks with the potential for serious injury or even death.

Example:

 Deciding to fly on a marginal day with very strong wind. You haven't flown for ages, it's winter, but the sun is out, high pressure is in... tempting, right? But the wind is gusty and powerful. Launching in these conditions, in my view, is knowingly taking a Type 1 risk.

Type 2 Risks: The Adventure Factors


These are risks that are not necessarily life-threatening (beyond the inherent baseline risk of simply being airborne) but involve inconvenience, challenge, and a sense of adventure.

Example: 

It's a beautiful spring day with a high cloudbase and lovely conditions. You decide to fly cross-country, deep into "the boonies." The primary risk here might be not finding that crucial thermal on a distant hillside, leading to a land-out and a long walk.

The Laziness Factor: A Hidden Influence?


Here's an interesting wrinkle: our inherent human tendency towards, let's be honest, laziness, can muddy the waters. Sometimes, to avoid the effort of a Type 2 risk (like a walk-out), we might subconsciously or even consciously opt for a higher Type 1 risk. Consider these scenarios:

The "Convenient" Landing: 

End of a long XC flight, you're tired. There's a big, safe landing field 1km out from the train station, or a tight, technical spot right around the corner. Choosing the tight landing – was it really worth all that extra Type 1 risk just to avoid a short walk?

Scratching to the Car:

 You're ridge soaring, and things get a bit light and scratchy. You're actually a ways from where you parked. Do you make a safe slope land or squeak in a top landing and accept the walk, or do you try to scratch all the way back to your car, weaving around other pilots also desperately trying to hang on in the lull? Each option beyond the safe landing takes significantly more risk, for quite dubious gains.

The Unplanned XC Dilemma: 

You're on a day you weren't planning to go XC, but you've connected with a great climb and are drifting over the back. You don't have a retrieve organised. The Type 2 risk is a potential hitchhike back to your car. Do you push on and embrace the adventure (and potential inconvenience), or do you turn back and stay on the hill, possibly missing a fantastic flight due to fear of that Type 2 effort?

Where the Lines Blur – And How to Manage Them

Of course, the lines can blur. Flying into the boonies without a phone signal can quickly drift from a Type 2 into a Type 1 risk if something goes wrong. This is where we can proactively manage and mitigate risk by carrying appropriate communication (like a satellite device) and safety gear, and continuously improving our skills.

Feeding the Need, Safely – And Overcoming Laziness

I believe we can find similar thrills by thoughtfully taking Type 2 risks. Landing out in the boonies, with good comms and a safe landing, is low-risk for serious injury but high-reward for experience and memories.

To become a successful XC pilot, you often need to learn to manage this "laziness factor" and actively embrace well-managed Type 2 risks. They are part of the journey and the growth. It's about accepting that a bit of extra effort or inconvenience is often the price of a great adventure.

In Conclusion: Think About Your Risks (and Your Laziness!)

So, if you’re a pilot who, like me, gets a buzz from pushing boundaries, I encourage you to consciously think about the kinds of risks you’re taking:

Categorise your risks: 

Are they predominantly Type 1 (potential for serious harm) or Type 2 (potential for inconvenience/adventure)?

Minimise Type 1:

 Actively work to reduce your exposure to Type 1 risks. Make smart decisions, don't exceed your skill level, and respect the conditions.

Mitigate what you can't eliminate: 

Carry the right safety kit and always have a plan.

Embrace (sensible) Type 2 risks and challenge laziness: 

Don't shy away from adventures that stretch you a bit, even if they involve some effort. These are often the flights that build crucial experience and satisfy that inner thrill-seeker.

Be honest about your motivations: 

Ask yourself: "Am I taking this (Type 1) risk to avoid a (Type 2) inconvenience?" To be a safe, "old not bold" pilot, you need to rigorously manage and avoid unnecessary Type 1 risks, even if it means a bit more effort. Conversely, to grow as a pilot, especially in XC, you often need to accept and even relish those Type 2 inconveniences.

By thoughtfully categorising and managing our risks – and being honest about our own tendencies – we can continue to enjoy the incredible freedom and excitement of paragliding for many years to come.

Fly smart, fly safe, and may your adventures be thrilling (and your walks-out character-building!)

Don’t forget to Strava your walk outs, or they didn’t happen

sections.title: PG Tips 🫖 (Paragliding tips)
sections.content:
Theo de Blic’s tip for throwing your reserve

I strongly recommend keeping both brake toggles in one hand and held down deeply and throwing with the other hand so the wing doesn’t ‘restart’ or violently pitch forward just as you go to throw.  By keeping your wing stalled, you greatly reduce the chance of throwing the reserve into the wing.  (The exception to this is if you’re in an autorotation or locked-in-spiral, in which case you might not be able to stall the wing and it’s more important to focus on a clean throw away from the wing.)

Extracted from Advanced Paragliding by Gavin McClurg, available from the club library.

sections.title: Skew T - For You and Me! (Part 2 - Reading Thermals)
sections.content:
Jordan Whittaker reports

Hi everyone, I hope part 1 was useful about how to read the skew-T. If you missed it and are completely new to it, I’d recommend looking at last month’s Skywords edition.

This month we’re looking at thermals, specifically how to read the Skew-T’s forecast for their strength and how regular they are. So let’s dive in:

Example 1:

This is a pretty classic flying day example to look out for. Looking at the red line, showing temperature with altitude, the ground level air is 10 degrees, and it reduces at the same rate as the dry adiabatic lapse rate, DALR (the solid green lines). When the air trapped on the surface heats up a few degrees, and releases as a thermal, they will cool at the rate of the solid green line (parallel in this case to the environmental lapse rate, the red line). It will continue cooling and rising until it hits the mixing ratio line (dashed pinky), which comes from the base of the blue line (the dew point).

For clarity, I’ve drawn in black dashed lines showing where to look and where they meet at the black dot. At this point, a cloud forms. If you go across to the y axis, you’ll be able to figure out which altitude cloud base correlates to for that day!

The pocket of air which has now formed to be a cumulus will continue to rise and cool, this time at a new rate. Rather than cooling along the DALR (solid green) line, it will cool and rise along the saturated adiabatic lapse rate, SALR (dashed green line), as seen in my black dashed line. It cools until it meets the environmental temperature (red line) where the lack of temperature difference causes no further rising. This is your typical cloud top, so you can figure out that altitude and see how beefy those clouds will be!

Something important which I want to point out is that at the beginning, the air is rising along the DALR line (solid green) and the gap between that and the red line remains fairly constant. Remember that temperature difference is what determines how fast the thermal rises, so if this temperature difference remains constant, the thermal will be rising at a fairly consistent speed upwards, probably pretty smoothly!

Example 2:

In this example, notice the yellow shaded area is more of a wedge shape, with a larger gap at the bottom and getting narrower near the top. For this example, when a thermal releases, it’ll be stronger, but as it rises and the gap between the lines decreases, it’ll become weaker.

Example 3:

In this example, notice the yellow shaded area is the opposite of example 2, this time it’s getting wider apart as it rises. On a day like this, you can expect thermals to begin more gently and get stronger with height, which will also add to turbulence. On my recent flight from Bradwell to Bridlington (had to drop this in there), I was really low after crossing the humber and unsure if I’d hit 100km. I found a slow 0m/s thermal and just stayed with it to get some extra distance, and it slowly grew into 0.5 m/s, 1, 2 and even 3 m/s where it leveled out until I got back to cloud base. A climb like this would probably be found when the Skew T looks like this example! You can also expect thermals to come more regularly since the red line drops off quickly from the DALR (green line). Not much temperature difference will be required for a pocket of air to suddenly be warmer than the surrounding environment, meaning thermals will occur more regularly.

So next time you’re out flying on a 5* day, take a quick look at some skew T’s and try to picture how thermals will be using the 3 examples I gave above, whether they’ll be slow and smooth the whole way up, or if they’ll be accelerating the whole way up in a bumpy ride!
